summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether HTML validation is worth the effort, noting that nearly all major websites fail W3C validation yet function perfectly well. After validating Stack Overflow as HTML 4.01 strict, he found the process caught some genuine errors but also required pointless changes and arbitrary rule compliance. He dismisses XHTML validation as masochistic complexity without benefit. His conclusion is characteristically pragmatic: understand validation and what valid HTML means, but don't let perfect validation distract from building useful features and running your website.
key_points: Nearly every major website fails HTML validation, including Google, Microsoft, and Stack Overflow itself, yet they all work fine
XHTML offers no compelling advantage over HTML while adding complexity and uncertainty
Some validation rules are actively harmful, like forbidding the target attribute and requiring JavaScript workarounds
Validation has value as a strict lint-like tool that catches genuine errors and teaches you the official rules
Valid HTML matters far less than delivering features and running your website effectively
money_quotes: The web is, to put it charitably, a rather forgiving place.
The whole HTML validation exercise is questionable, but validating as XHTML is flat-out masochism. Only recommended for those that enjoy pain. Or programmers. I can't always tell the difference.
XHTML offers no compelling advantage – to me – over HTML, but even if it did it would also offer increased complexity and uncertainty that make it unappealing to me.

I couldn't help feeling that validating as HTML 4.01 strict, at least in our case, was a giant exercise in to-may-to versus to-mah-to, punctuated by aggravating changes that we were forced to make for no practical benefit.
Learning the rules of the validator, even if you don't agree with them, teaches you what the official definition of 'valid' is.
Nobody cares if your HTML is valid. Except you. If you want to.
Take my advice and do as you please.
key_insight: Understanding validation rules has educational value, but pragmatic developers should prioritize shipping useful features over achieving perfect standards compliance that no user will ever notice.
